skill.name: managing-branches
skill.description: Plan and automate Git branching workflows, including Gitflow, GitHub Flow, trunk-based development, release branches, hotfixes, and worktrees.
skill.summary: Manage Git branch strategy, naming, flow validation, and worktree operations.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, network, and weak-cryptography patterns. Review confirmed that most hits are Git documentation examples or false positives, but the packaged Python scripts execute git commands and can change local branches, remotes, configuration files, and worktrees. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious network behavior was found, so publication is acceptable with clear warnings.

medium_findings.title: Git Command Execution in Helper Scripts
medium_findings.description: The Python helpers wrap subprocess.run to execute git commands for branch, merge, cleanup, validation, and worktree operations. The calls use argument arrays instead of shell=True, which reduces injection risk, but the skill can still modify repository state when users run the scripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess.run calls are real and central to the skill. Confidence is high for command execution capability, but lower than critical because commands are git-focused and not shell-concatenated.

medium_findings.title: Configurable Worktree Paths and File Writes
medium_findings.description: The skill writes a branching configuration file and supports worktree base directories that default outside the repository. This is expected for Git worktree automation, but users should review configured paths before creating worktrees.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is directly visible in script defaults and config writes. It appears legitimate, but it can write outside the current repository if configured that way.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Branch and Worktree Cleanup Operations
medium_findings.description: Cleanup commands can delete local branches, delete remote branches, and remove worktrees after confirmation or with yes and force flags. This is functional branch management behavior, but it can cause data loss if used on the wrong repository or with unreviewed arguments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The deletion commands are explicit and implemented through git. Confirmation checks reduce risk, but the destructive behavior is real.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples Flagged as Execution
low_findings.description: Many static findings are Markdown examples for git, gh, shell loops, and branch workflow commands. They are not executed by the skill unless a user copies or asks to run them, so they are documentation risk rather than hidden execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced examples or workflow documentation. This strongly supports a false-positive classification for most Markdown execution hits.

low_findings.title: Localhost URLs and GitHub CLI Text Flagged as Network Risk
low_findings.description: The network findings are localhost browser examples and GitHub CLI workflow examples. I did not find evidence of hidden HTTP requests, credential upload, or external data exfiltration in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL evidence points to localhost comparison instructions, and the gh usage is normal repository workflow text. No network client code was found in the scripts.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: Weak cryptography findings appear to be triggered by words and short tokens in descriptions, branch naming, or argparse help text. I did not find cryptographic API usage or intentionally weak hashing logic in the reviewed script context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are descriptions, output strings, or parser text rather than crypto operations. This makes the static weak-crypto category likely false positive.

low_findings.title: Hidden File Access Is Git Configuration Guidance
low_findings.description: The hidden-file findings are examples that append worktree patterns to a global gitignore file. This is normal Git setup documentation, but it should remain visible to users before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is a documented gitignore setup example, not covert access to hidden files. It is still worth noting because it affects user-level Git configuration.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run Wrapper for Git Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts centralize Git command execution through subprocess.run. Argument-array usage avoids shell command substitution, but commands still operate on the user repository.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly invokes subprocess.run in all three scripts. Risk is bounded because the executable is git and shell=True is not used.

dangerous_patterns.title: Relative Worktree Base Directory
dangerous_patterns.description: Default worktree configuration uses ../worktrees, so generated worktree paths are intentionally outside the repository root. This is common for Git worktrees, but users should approve path changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The relative path appears repeatedly in templates and scripts. It is a legitimate pattern with filesystem impact rather than evidence of malicious traversal.

dangerous_patterns.title: Branch and Worktree Removal Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The cleanup functionality includes git branch deletion, remote branch deletion, and git worktree remove operations. These actions are dangerous if run without checking branch state first.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and destructive. Built-in prompts lower the risk, but the behavior remains security-relevant for marketplace users.

content.value_statement: Branching strategy drift can slow releases and confuse teams. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code guide branch naming, flow choices, validation, cleanup, and worktree use.
actual_capabilities: Explains Gitflow, GitHub Flow, GitLab Flow, and trunk-based branching tradeoffs.
Generates branch naming guidance for features, bugfixes, hotfixes, releases, docs, and refactors.
Provides scripts for branch creation, branch finishing, status checks, cleanup, and configuration.
Provides scripts for Git worktree creation, listing, removal, cleanup, and status reporting.
Validates branch names, merge direction, and flow compliance against a branching configuration.
Includes configuration templates for common branching strategies and repository policies.
limitations: The scripts operate on the local Git repository and can change branch state.
It does not replace repository permissions, branch protection, or CI policy enforcement.
Worktree path settings can create directories outside the repository when configured that way.
Remote branch cleanup requires careful review before use on shared repositories.

use_cases.title: Choose a Branching Strategy
use_cases.description: Compare Gitflow, GitHub Flow, GitLab Flow, and trunk-based development for team size, release cadence, and deployment model.

use_cases.title: Standardize Branch Naming
use_cases.description: Create naming rules and validation checks for feature, bugfix, hotfix, release, documentation, and refactor branches.

use_cases.title: Work in Parallel Safely
use_cases.description: Use Git worktrees to handle hotfixes, reviews, releases, and feature work without constantly switching branches.

prompt_templates.title: Pick a Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a Git branching strategy for a team of [team size] that releases [cadence] and deploys to [environments].

prompt_templates.title: Create a Branch Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Design branch naming, base branches, merge targets, and cleanup rules for [repository type] using [Gitflow, GitHub Flow, or trunk-based development].

prompt_templates.title: Validate Current Branches
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my branch list and identify naming, merge direction, stale branch, and release process problems. Ask before suggesting destructive cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Automate Worktree Usage
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a safe worktree workflow for hotfixes, pull request review, and release stabilization, including confirmation points before deletion.

output_examples.input: We release every two weeks and maintain one production version. Which strategy should we use?
output_examples.output: A concise recommendation comparing GitHub Flow and trunk-based development, with branch roles, merge rules, release tagging, and protection settings.

output_examples.input: Review these branch names: Feature/Login, hotfix/security-patch, release/2.4.0, bug_123.
output_examples.output: A naming review that marks valid branches, explains invalid names, and suggests lowercase, hyphenated replacements.

output_examples.input: I need to fix production while keeping my feature work open.
output_examples.output: A worktree plan that creates an isolated hotfix workspace, explains base branch selection, and includes cleanup checks after merge.

best_practices: Review the target repository and branch protection rules before running automation scripts.
Use dry-run, confirmation prompts, and pull requests before branch or worktree cleanup.
Keep one branching strategy per repository and document exceptions for releases or hotfixes.
anti_patterns: Do not run cleanup commands on shared repositories without reviewing every branch listed.
Do not configure worktree paths to sensitive or unrelated directories.
Do not bypass branch protection by treating local automation as policy enforcement.

faq.question: Can this skill run Git commands?
faq.answer: Yes. The included Python scripts can run Git commands when a user or agent executes them.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is acceptable with warnings. The reviewed evidence shows legitimate Git automation, not malicious exfiltration.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Can it enforce branch protection in GitHub?
faq.answer: No. It can advise on policies, but repository settings and permissions must enforce protection.

faq.question: Can it delete branches?
faq.answer: Yes. Cleanup helpers can delete merged local branches and attempt remote deletion after confirmation or explicit flags.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill has real command and filesystem effects, but review found no malicious intent or hidden network exfiltration.
